summary: This technical deep-dive explains how the Zig compiler converts its Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) into Zig Intermediate Representation (ZIR) through a stage called AstGen. ZIR is an untyped intermediate format that breaks program logic into fine-grained instructions, preparing code for comptime evaluation and further semantic analysis. The post meticulously covers ZIR instruction anatomy, including how references work, extra data encoding, and the role of scopes and string interning. It concludes by walking through progressively complex examples—from integer literals to addition to variable assignment—demonstrating how the recursive generation process builds up ZIR from simpler components.
key_points: ZIR is untyped because Zig's comptime evaluation means types may not be known until later stages—types are first-class values that can be computed dynamically
ZIR instructions use a compact encoding scheme with tagged refs for common primitives and instruction indices for everything else, enabling efficient memory usage
AstGen introduces the first significant layer of semantic validation, checking that identifiers are defined and don't shadow outer scopes
Scopes are polymorphic structures that enable identifier resolution by traversing parent scopes, with seven different scope types serving different purposes
The recursive generation process builds complex constructs from simpler ones—understanding integer literals helps understand addition, which helps understand assignment
money_quotes: Whereas the AST is a tree, the IR usually begins creating a sequence of instructions for various blocks of a program.
A language-defining feature of Zig is that it uses types as first-class values and comptime-evaluation as a mechanism for generic typing.
ZIR is the prepared intermediary form for comptime evaluation and further semantic analysis.
After AstGen, the AST and source can be freed. This enables parsing extremely large Zig programs and storing only what we need in memory.
AstGen introduces our first significant layer of semantic validation.
I find it easiest to start with the simplest components of a language and then build up from there, since IR generation is a deeply recursive process.
If I started with something like a function declaration, I'd get bogged down in so many details that it'd be overwhelming to learn.
key_insight: Understanding compiler internals becomes manageable when you start with the simplest constructs and recursively build up complexity, mirroring how the compiler itself processes code.
